findings.title: Hardcoded AWS access key in test fixture
findings.evidence: The raw diff adds a single line: `export const fakeAwsKey = "AKIA0123456789ABCDEF";`. The AKIA prefix is the standard AWS access key ID format. The value is exported from the module, meaning any importer — or a secret-scanning tool watching the repo — will see it as a live-looking credential. Diff Drift flags it at high severity for the same reason.

notes: The variable name 'fakeAwsKey' and the fixture file path suggest developer intent is to use this as a test constant, not a real credential. However, the AKIA-prefixed value matches the exact format AWS validates, so scanners (GitHub secret scanning, truffleHog, etc.) will alert on it in any public or enterprise repo. Best practice is to use a clearly invalid placeholder (e.g., 'AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE' from AWS docs, or a non-AKIA string) or load the value from an environment variable even in tests.
The file is a new addition (previously non-existent), so there is no regression risk — but the hardcoded value should be replaced before merging.

evaluator.note: Benchmark v4 multi-model panel; blind, packet-only context.
